b1-experiential.title: The Experiential ("Have You Ever...?")
b1-experiential.explanation: To ask or state whether someone has ever had the experience of doing something, attach the converb suffix -п (or -ып/-іп after a consonant) to the verb stem, followed by a form of көру ("to see/try"). Literally "to try seeing [having done] X," this construction — жеп көрдің бе? — asks "have you ever eaten [this]?"
b1-experiential.fact: The verb көру ("to see") doing double duty as "to try/experience" is a pattern found elsewhere in Kazakh too — much like English "try seeing what happens," Kazakh treats trying an experience as a kind of "seeing" it for yourself.

dialogue.native: Сен бұрын самса пісіріп көрдің бе?
dialogue.en: Have you ever tried baking samsa?
No, but I would like to try.

b1-habitual-past.title: The Habitual Past ("Used To")
b1-habitual-past.explanation: To describe a repeated or habitual action in the past — the equivalent of English "used to" — attach -атын (back-vowel stem), -етін (front-vowel stem), or -йтын (after a vowel) to the verb stem, then typically add a personal ending or a form of еді: ойнайтынмын (I used to play). This frames the action as a repeated habit, not a single completed event.
b1-habitual-past.fact: The same -атын/-етін suffix also builds the indirect future/present participle used in reported speech ("қашан келетінін білмеймін" — "I don't know when he will come"), so this one ending covers both "used to happen regularly" and "is expected to happen," depending on context.

dialogue.native: Сен жас кезіңде спортпен айналысатынсың ба?
dialogue.en: Did you use to do sports when you were young?
Yes, I used to go swimming.

b1-necessity-degrees.title: Degrees of Necessity (керек, тиіс, қажет)
b1-necessity-degrees.explanation: Kazakh has three overlapping words for obligation, each with a different shade of meaning. Керек is neutral and everyday, roughly "need to/should" (баруым керек — I need to go). Тиіс carries a stronger sense of duty, common in formal or written contexts ("must, is obliged to"). Қажет is closer to "necessary," often used impersonally to describe a general requirement rather than a personal duty.
b1-necessity-degrees.fact: The choice between these three words is a reliable register signal — a spoken request will almost always use керек, a law or regulation will lean on тиіс, and a report describing what a situation "requires" will usually reach for қажет.

dialogue.en: What do I need to do?
First you need to prepare the documents.

items.kk: Сен жылқы еті жеп көрдің бе?
items.en: Have you ever tried horse meat?
I have been to Japan.

items.kk: Ол ешқашан ұшақпен ұшып көрген емес.
items.en: He has never flown by plane before.
We have watched this film.
Have you ever tried kumys?

items.kk: Мен бұрын мұны істеп көрген емеспін.
items.en: I have never done this before.
Have they ever swum in the sea?
I have tried playing the dombra.
Have you ever tried writing in Kazakh?

items.kk: Ол бұрын мұндай тағам жеп көрмеген.
items.en: He has never eaten such food before.
When I was young, I used to play football.

items.kk: Ол бұрын күн сайын кітап оқитын.
items.en: He used to read a book every day before.
We used to live in the village.
What did you used to do as a child?
My mother used to tell me fairy tales.
They used to swim together.
I used to get up early.
She used to sing songs.
We used to travel on weekends.
He used to study well at school.
I need to go to the library.
Citizens must obey the law.
It is necessary to solve this problem.
Students are obliged to complete their homework.
I need to drink water.
It is necessary to see a doctor.
He must keep his word.
You need to rest.
Additional information is necessary.
Workers must follow the rules.
